query: effectiveness of liraglutide for weight reduction

retrieved_docs.title: Obesity Medications: Evidence-Based Management -- Function -- Liraglutide
retrieved_docs.content: Liraglutide (Saxenda)  is a daily GLP-1 receptor agonist that pioneered incretin-based obesity therapy (see Table 3 ). Please refer to StatPearls' companion resource, " Pharmacologic Therapy for Obesity ," for additional information. While offering more modest weight loss compared to newer weekly agents, liraglutide maintains clinical relevance through its established safety profile, pediatric indication for adolescents 12 years and older, and flexibility for dose adjustments. The SCALE clinical trial program demonstrated a 5.4% to 8.0% weight loss at 56 weeks, with 63.2% of participants achieving a weight loss of 5% or greater.

retrieved_docs.title: Compare and Contrast the Glucagon-Like Peptide-1 Receptor Agonists (GLP1RAs) -- Mechanism of Action -- Numerous clinical trials and studies have been conducted between the short-acting and long-acting GLP-1 analogs to gauge their efficacy, safety, and tolerability. In addition, the added benefit of weight loss and improved cardiovascular and renal outcomes was also assessed. Efficacy and adverse effects
retrieved_docs.content: One meta-analysis showed that once-weekly GLP-1RA were more effective in reducing HbA1c levels and equally as effective in weight loss than exenatide twice-daily injection, but not better than liraglutide once-daily injection. [11] To further support this, a study showed that long-acting GLP-1RA like dulaglutide, liraglutide, and once-weekly exenatide were superior to exenatide twice daily and lixisenatide at lowering fasting plasma glucose levels and HbA1c levels of patients. [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Liraglutide -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: The American Association of Clinical Endocrinologists and American College of Endocrinology (AACE/ACE) Obesity Clinical Practice Guidelines suggest that patients with obesity should be offered chronic treatment using weight loss medications and lifestyle modifications, provided that the potential benefits outweigh the risks of adverse reactions. Liraglutide is generally not recommended as short-term therapy as this treatment duration is not associated with long-term health benefits.

retrieved_docs.title: Pharmacologic Therapy for Obesity -- Clinical Significance -- Liraglutide
retrieved_docs.content: Randomized controlled trials have demonstrated liraglutide's efficacy in diabetes management and weight loss. Multiple studies have shown sustained weight loss and additional cardiovascular benefits over a maximum duration of 2 years.

retrieved_docs.title: Compare and Contrast the Glucagon-Like Peptide-1 Receptor Agonists (GLP1RAs) -- Mechanism of Action -- Weight loss
retrieved_docs.content: Recently, weekly semaglutide 2.4 mg became the first approved drug for chronic weight management in overweight adults or those with general obesity with at least one weight-related comorbidity since the approval of high-dose liraglutide in 2014. Compared to the SCALE trial comparing weight management in liraglutide versus placebo, the 4 randomized controlled trials comparing semaglutide to placebo showed a greater average weight loss, resulting in a more significant percentage of patients losing at least 5% of their body weight.

retrieved_docs.title: Pharmacologic Therapy for Obesity -- Clinical Significance -- Liraglutide
retrieved_docs.content: In a 20-week study involving 564 patients, liraglutide at 4 different daily doses (1.2, 1.8, 2.4, and 3 mg) showed dose-dependent weight loss compared to placebo or open-label orlistat, with weight losses of 5.8 kg and 3.8 kg more than placebo and orlistat, respectively. These results were consistent in a 2-year extension study, where patients on higher doses (2.4 mg and 3.0 mg) maintained a weight loss of 7.8 kg at the end of the study (total n=92 patients on the higher doses). Notably, liraglutide 3.0 mg also substantially reduced body fat by 15.4% while preserving lean tissue, which decreased by only 2.0%. Moreover, the prevalence of prediabetes and metabolic syndrome was reduced by 52% and 59%, respectively, after 2 years, accompanied by improved blood pressure and lipid levels. [25]

retrieved_docs.title: Liraglutide -- Administration -- Adult Dosage
retrieved_docs.content: For weight loss: The recommended adult dose is 3 mg daily. Patients should start with an initial dose of 0.6 mg daily for the first week and increase the dose by 0.6 mg every week until the target dose of 3 mg is achieved. An outline of the dosing schedule for weight loss is as follows: Week 1: 0.6 mg daily Week 2: 1.2 mg daily Week 3: 1.8 mg daily Week 4: 2.4 mg daily Week 5+: 3 mg daily

retrieved_docs.title: Liraglutide -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Diagnosing and treating type 2 diabetes requires regular collaboration within the healthcare team. Prescribers and pharmacists work together to ensure patients receive their medication at a reasonable cost and within an appropriate time frame. Continuous monitoring of patients' glucose levels assesses the effect of liraglutide on glycemic control. Primary care physicians and advanced-level providers diagnose and treat T2D and manage weight loss. In severe cases where glycemic control is not achieved with first- or second-line treatments, an endocrinologist may be consulted to further evaluate the patient's endocrine dysfunction. For weight loss, patients may require medications such as liraglutide to increase metabolism and reduce appetite. Pharmacists and prescribers communicate with patients to navigate insurance challenges, explore patient assistance programs, and consider alternative payment options to improve access to these medications. Nurses ensure patients understand safe and precise dose administration and drug interactions and educate patients on lifestyle and diet modifications when indicated. Patients taking liraglutide should follow up with their providers every 3 to 6 months to evaluate glycemic control or weight loss progress. Patients with diabetes require close surveillance due to severe complications such as irreversible diabetic neuropathy. Patients taking liraglutide maintain regular appointments and monitor their blood glucose levels. Clinicians, pharmacists, and dieticians work collaboratively to ensure optimal use of liraglutide to treat obesity and T2D.

retrieved_docs.title: Liraglutide -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Liraglutide is a glucagon-like peptide-1 receptor agonist (GLP-1 RA) used to treat type 2 diabetes (T2D) and obesity. This drug belongs to the incretin mimetic class of antidiabetic agents and acts on pancreatic β-cells to stimulate the release of endogenous insulin, helping to control blood glucose levels and suppress appetite. The FDA approved liraglutide in 2010 as a treatment for T2D and in 2014 for weight loss. This activity reviews liraglutide's mechanism of action, dosing, pharmacodynamics, pharmacokinetics, adverse event profile, off-label uses, monitoring, and relevant interactions. These aspects of liraglutide are critical for healthcare professionals managing patients with T2D and related conditions. Understanding liraglutide's proper indications and potential side effects supports effective communication across the interprofessional healthcare team.

retrieved_docs.title: Compare and Contrast the Glucagon-Like Peptide-1 Receptor Agonists (GLP1RAs) -- Mechanism of Action -- Numerous clinical trials and studies have been conducted between the short-acting and long-acting GLP-1 analogs to gauge their efficacy, safety, and tolerability. In addition, the added benefit of weight loss and improved cardiovascular and renal outcomes was also assessed. Efficacy and adverse effects
retrieved_docs.content: In the AWARD-1 trial, dulaglutide was superior to exenatide twice-daily injection. Further trials followed this up, and in the AWARD 6 trial, once-weekly dulaglutide was shown to be as effective as once-weekly liraglutide. Liraglutide, however, had more significant weight loss potential. [16] In another trial, dulaglutide, when compared to liraglutide, had a greater HbA1c reduction. [17] When dulaglutide's initiation in patients was compared to exenatide once weekly and liraglutide, dulaglutide had a higher adherence rate and lower discontinuation rates within 6 months. [18] A more recent randomized controlled trial called SUSTAIN-7 compared semaglutide once-weekly injection to dulaglutide at both low and high doses. Semaglutide showed superiority over dulaglutide in improving glycemic control and weight loss >5%, with both drugs having a similar safety profile. [19]

retrieved_docs.title: Compare and Contrast the Glucagon-Like Peptide-1 Receptor Agonists (GLP1RAs) -- Mechanism of Action -- Numerous clinical trials and studies have been conducted between the short-acting and long-acting GLP-1 analogs to gauge their efficacy, safety, and tolerability. In addition, the added benefit of weight loss and improved cardiovascular and renal outcomes was also assessed. Efficacy and adverse effects
retrieved_docs.content: Another meta-analysis demonstrated that liraglutide decreased HbA1c more than exenatide, and there was no difference in HbA1c reduction between liraglutide and dulaglutide. The adverse effects were mainly gastrointestinal, with less nausea seen in exenatide once-weekly injection, and most adverse effects were seen with short-acting agents. [13] The LEAD-6 trial compared liraglutide 1.8mg once a day with exenatide 10 mcg twice a day, and it showed a greater HbA1c reduction and fewer adverse effects with liraglutide, including hypoglycemia. [14] In DURATION-6, liraglutide once daily was compared to exenatide once weekly, and liraglutide showed a significant reduction in both HbA1c and weight. However, more GI side effects were associated with using liraglutide. Compared to exenatide once-weekly injection, exenatide twice-daily injection showed a greater reduction of HbA1c level; however, there were more injection site reactions. [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Liraglutide -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the appropriate indications for liraglutide administration. Determine the appropriate dosage adjustments for liraglutide if necessary. Identify the adverse effects associated with liraglutide administration. Implement effective collaboration and communication among interprofessional team members to improve outcomes and treatment efficacy for patients who might benefit from liraglutide therapy. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Non-Dieting Approaches to Treatment of Obesity -- Issues of Concern -- 2. Liraglutide
retrieved_docs.content: Approximate weight loss in % total body weight: 4 to 5.4

retrieved_docs.title: Compare and Contrast the Glucagon-Like Peptide-1 Receptor Agonists (GLP1RAs) -- Mechanism of Action -- Numerous clinical trials and studies have been conducted between the short-acting and long-acting GLP-1 analogs to gauge their efficacy, safety, and tolerability. In addition, the added benefit of weight loss and improved cardiovascular and renal outcomes was also assessed. Efficacy and adverse effects
retrieved_docs.content: Cardiovascular outcomes Several cardiovascular outcome trials have been done on the GLP-1RA. The LEADER-6 trial showed that liraglutide had a cardiovascular benefit in high-risk patients. The SUSTAIN 6 trial demonstrated a statistically significant reduction in death from cardiovascular events with the use of subcutaneous semaglutide; however, it also had the highest decrease in HbA1c, so it was unclear whether the reduction of death from CV events was from the medication or the improved glycemic control. Out of the 7 trials, all have shown noninferiority, and liraglutide, subcutaneous semaglutide, and dulaglutide have significantly reduced cardiovascular outcomes. [20] According to a post hoc analysis of the SUSTAIN and PIONEER trials, semaglutide has shown a decreased relative risk and absolute risk reduction for major adverse cardiovascular events versus its comparators. [21] The AHA emphasizes reducing ASCVD-related outcomes like MI, stroke, and cardiovascular death and recommends GLP-1 receptor agonists such as liraglutide, subcutaneous semaglutide, dulaglutide, and albiglutide, which is currently not available in the US or EU, for their established cardiovascular benefits. [22]

retrieved_docs.title: Pharmacologic Therapy for Obesity -- Clinical Significance -- Liraglutide
retrieved_docs.content: The LEADER trial showed that liraglutide, even at a lower dose than the recommended dosage for obesity (1.8 mg versus 3 mg), reduced major cardiovascular events, including cardiovascular-related deaths, nonfatal myocardial infarction, or nonfatal stroke, in patients with type 2 diabetes. [27] However, there is currently no available cardiovascular outcome data for patients with obesity who do not have diabetes.

retrieved_docs.title: Compare and Contrast the Glucagon-Like Peptide-1 Receptor Agonists (GLP1RAs) -- Indications -- FDA-approved GLP-1 agonists for weight loss include:
retrieved_docs.content: Semaglutide SC Liraglutide SC [1]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 3 -- Community 2063
retrieved_docs.content: Glucagon-like peptide‑1 (GLP‑1) analogues constitute a class of therapeutic agents that mimic the incretin hormone GLP‑1, enhancing glucose‑dependent insulin secretion, inhibiting glucagon release, slowing gastric emptying, and promoting satiety. Liraglutide is a member of this GLP‑1 analogue class, and its pharmacologic actions make it valuable for the treatment of type 2 diabetes mellitus and for weight management in patients with obesity.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 2 -- Community 888
retrieved_docs.content: Glucagon‑like peptide‑1 (GLP‑1) analogues comprise several agents, including exenatid, liraglutide, and lixisenatide. These GLP‑1 analogues are often combined with basal insulins to enhance glycaemic control in patients with diabetes. The fixed‑ratio combination product insulin degludec / liraglutide incorporates liraglutide, while the combination insulin glargine / lixisenatide contains lixisenatide. By pairing GLP‑1 receptor agonists such as liraglutide or lixisenatide with long‑acting insulins, clinicians can exploit complementary mechanisms—insulin’s basal glucose‑lowering effect and GLP‑1 analogues’ glucose‑dependent insulin secretion, delayed gastric emptying, and appetite suppression—to achieve more effective and convenient diabetes management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 2 -- Community 711
retrieved_docs.content: Glucagon‑like peptide‑1 (GLP‑1) analogues are a class of antidiabetic agents that includes both exenatid and liraglutid. In addition to being a member of the GLP‑1 analogue class, liraglutid is incorporated into the fixed‑dose combination product insulin degludec / liraglutide, which pairs basal insulin degludec with the GLP‑1 analogue liraglutid to provide complementary mechanisms for glucose lowering. This relationship highlights how individual GLP‑1 analogues can serve both as standalone therapies and as components of combination regimens designed to enhance glycemic control in patients with diabetes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 1152
retrieved_docs.content: Liraglutide is classified as a member of the glucagon‑like peptide‑1 (GLP‑1) analogue class, and the fixed‑dose combination product insulin degludec/liraglutide includes liraglutide as an integral component of its formulation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 723
retrieved_docs.content: Glucagon‑like peptide‑1 (GLP‑1) analogues comprise the agents exenatide, liraglutide and lixisenatide. Liraglutide is a component of the fixed‑dose combination insulin degludec / liraglutide, while lixisenatide is incorporated into the fixed‑dose combination insulin glargine / lixisenatide. The category “Insulins and analogues for injection, long‑acting” includes insulin glargine, the combination insulin degludec / liraglutide, and the combination insulin glargine / lixisenatide. These relationships illustrate how GLP‑1 receptor agonists are used both as individual therapies and as integral parts of long‑acting basal insulin combinations, providing complementary mechanisms to improve glycaemic control in patients with diabetes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 1 -- Community 267
retrieved_docs.content: insulin degludec/liraglutide contains the GLP‑1 analogue liraglutide, and insulin glargine/lixisenatide contains the GLP‑1 analogue lixisenatide. This integration of an insulin with a GLP‑1 analogue provides both basal insulin coverage and incretin‑mediated glucose control in a single injection. Overall, the taxonomy clarifies how diabetes pharmacotherapy is organized: insulin‑based therapies are distinguished from non‑insulin glucose‑lowering agents, and within each group, drugs are further classified by mechanism, duration of action, and formulation, facilitating precise selection and combination of therapies for optimal patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 772
retrieved_docs.content: Glucagon-like peptide‑1 (GLP‑1) analogues are a class of incretin‑based medications that include both exenatid and lixisenatide. Exenatid, as a member of the GLP‑1 analogue family, acts on the GLP‑1 receptor to enhance glucose‑dependent insulin secretion, suppress glucagon release, and slow gastric emptying. Lixisenatide similarly belongs to the GLP‑1 analogue class and shares these pharmacologic effects. In addition to being an individual GLP‑1 analogue, lixisenatide is incorporated into the fixed‑dose combination product insulin glargine/lixisenatide, which pairs basal insulin glargine with the GLP‑1 receptor agonist to provide combined basal insulin and incretin therapy for improved glycemic control in patients with type 2 diabetes. This integration underscores the therapeutic strategy of using GLP‑1 analogues both as standalone agents and as components of combination regimens to optimize blood glucose management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2351
retrieved_docs.content: Insulin degludec/liraglutide is a fixed‑dose combination medication in which liraglutide is a component, indicating that liraglutide is part of the insulin degludec/liraglutide formulation. This relationship clarifies that the drug contains both the basal insulin insulin degludec and the GLP‑1 receptor agonist liraglutide, providing dual mechanisms of action that are relevant for the management of type 2 diabetes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 1318
retrieved_docs.content: Centrally acting antiobesity products are a class of pharmacologic agents that target central nervous system pathways to reduce appetite and promote weight loss, and this class includes both setmelanotide and sibutramine.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 2310
retrieved_docs.content: Lixisenatide is classified as a glucagon‑like peptide‑1 (GLP‑1) analogue, belonging to the therapeutic class of GLP‑1 analogues that mimic the incretin hormone GLP‑1 to enhance glucose‑dependent insulin secretion, suppress glucagon release, and slow gastric emptying. As a member of this drug class, lixisenatide is employed in the management of type 2 diabetes mellitus, often in combination with other antidiabetic agents, and its pharmacologic actions are consistent with those of other GLP‑1 analogues.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1498
retrieved_docs.content: Centrally acting antiobesity products constitute a pharmacologic class that promotes weight loss by influencing central nervous system pathways involved in appetite and energy balance. This class includes the combination therapy bupropion / naltrexone, the melanocortin‑4 receptor agonist setmelanotide, and the serotonin‑norepinephrine reuptake inhibitor sibutramine. Bupropion / naltrexone works by modulating reward and satiety circuits, setmelanotide targets genetic forms of obesity through melanocortin signaling, and sibutramine reduces appetite by increasing synaptic concentrations of serotonin and norepinephrine. Together, these centrally acting agents illustrate the diverse mechanisms by which central pathways can be leveraged to treat obesity, highlighting the importance of individualized therapeutic selection based on efficacy, safety profile, and underlying patient characteristics.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 1 -- Community 112
retrieved_docs.content: organized under “blood‑glucose‑lowering drugs, excl. insulins” and include alpha‑glucosidase inhibitors, DPP‑4 inhibitors (alogliptin, sitagliptin), and GLP‑1 analogues (liraglutide, lixisenatide). A further subclass, biguanides, comprises metformin and fenformin, which share a common chemical scaffold and glucose‑lowering activity. These hierarchical relationships illustrate how glycerol is shared across diverse formulations and how diabetes medications are systematically categorized by mechanism, molecular target, and pharmacologic profile to guide clinical use and patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 2 -- Community 975
retrieved_docs.content: Glucagon‑like peptide‑1 (GLP‑1) analogues comprise agents such as liraglutide and lixisenatide. Liraglutide is incorporated into the fixed‑dose combination product insulin degludec / liraglutide, while lixisenatide is incorporated into the fixed‑dose combination product insulin glargine / lixisenatide. Both combination products, together with insulin glargine alone, are classified within the therapeutic group “Insulins and analogues for injection, long‑acting.” This illustrates how GLP‑1 receptor agonists are paired with basal insulin therapies to provide long‑acting injectable regimens that enhance glycemic control in patients with diabetes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 1 -- Community 84
retrieved_docs.content: The therapeutic landscape for diabetes is organized into two principal categories: insulins and analogues, and blood‑glucose‑lowering drugs that exclude insulins. Within the insulin subclass, formulation groups are defined. “Insulins and analogues for injection used in Diabetes, intermediate‑ or long‑acting combined with fast‑acting” comprises rapid‑acting analogue insulin aspart (human), which binds the human insulin receptor (INSR) and produces the physiologic effect of decreased lipolysis, reflecting an anabolic action on adipose tissue. “Insulins and analogues for injection, long‑acting” includes insulin glargine and the fixed‑dose combinations insulin degludec / liraglutide and insulin glargine / lixisenatide; both combinations integrate a basal insulin with a GLP‑1 analogue to enhance glycaemic control. “Insulin, Beef” is a protein entity classified within the same combined intermediate‑/long‑acting‑with‑fast‑acting group, illustrating the diversity of insulin sources.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 2 -- Community 884
retrieved_docs.content: Centrally acting antiobesity products and peripherally acting antiobesity products are both classified as antiobesity preparations, excl. diet products. The centrally acting antiobesity products category includes the agents setmelanotide and sibutramine, which achieve weight loss through mechanisms that act within the central nervous system. Peripherally acting antiobesity products represent a separate class that exerts its effects outside the central nervous system, although specific members are not listed here. Understanding the distinction between central and peripheral mechanisms of antiobesity preparations helps clinicians choose appropriate pharmacologic strategies for obesity management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 3 -- Community 2062
retrieved_docs.content: Insulin glargine and the combination product insulin degludec / liraglutide are both classified as members of the therapeutic group “Insulins and analogues for injection, long‑acting.” This category comprises long‑acting injectable insulin analogues that are used to provide basal insulin coverage in the management of diabetes, helping to maintain stable blood glucose levels over extended periods.
